'''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡}} found in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. It is a {{ill|Gunka|ja|軍歌}} (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to pacify regions such as {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }}. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middle[[Category:middle]] volume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}} in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}. While it fell into disuse after the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|ja|後土御門天皇}} (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}} for {{ill|Emperor Ninkō|ja|仁孝天皇}}'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌}} and {{ill|Tanka|ja|短歌}}.
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛}}, {{ill|Hichiriki|ja|篳篥}}, and {{ill|Wagoto|ja|和琴}}. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==

'''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡}} found in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. It is a {{ill|Gunka|ja|軍歌}} (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to pacify regions such as {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }}. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}} in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}. While it fell into disuse after the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|ja|後土御門天皇}} (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}} for {{ill|Emperor Ninkō|ja|仁孝天皇}}'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌}} and {{ill|Tanka|ja|短歌}}.
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛}}, {{ill|Hichiriki|ja|篳篥}}, and {{ill|Wagoto|ja|和琴}}. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==

revision.text:
'''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡}} found in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. It is a {{ill|Gunka|ja|軍歌}} (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to pacify regions such as {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }}. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}} in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}. While it fell into disuse after the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|ja|後土御門天皇}} (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}} for {{ill|Emperor Ninkō|ja|仁孝天皇}}'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌}} and {{ill|Tanka|ja|短歌}}.
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛}}, {{ill|Hichiriki|ja|篳篥}}, and {{ill|Wagoto|ja|和琴}}. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōkume no Mikoto|ja|大久米命}} * {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} * {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} * {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} * {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}

'''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡}} found in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. It is a {{ill|Gunka|ja|軍歌}} (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to pacify regions such as {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }}. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}} in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}. While it fell into disuse after the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|ja|後土御門天皇}} (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}} for {{ill|Emperor Ninkō|ja|仁孝天皇}}'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌}} and {{ill|Tanka|ja|短歌}}.
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛}}, {{ill|Hichiriki|ja|篳篥}}, and {{ill|Wagoto|ja|和琴}}. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōkume no Mikoto|ja|大久米命}} * {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} * {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} * {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} * {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}

The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tanka|ja|短歌|12=draft}}.
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|12=draft}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏|12=draft}}, who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|12=draft}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hichiriki|ja|篳篥|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Wagoto|ja|和琴|12=draft}}. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).

'''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡|12=draft}} found in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}. It is a {{ill|Gunka|ja|軍歌|12=draft}} (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=draft}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞|12=draft}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft}} to pacify regions such as {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}}. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=draft}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥|12=draft}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦|12=draft}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|12=draft}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺|12=draft}} in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft}}. While it fell into disuse after the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|ja|後土御門天皇|12=draft}} (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭|12=draft}} for {{ill|Emperor Ninkō|ja|仁孝天皇|12=draft}}'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|12=draft}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|12=draft}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|12=draft}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tanka|ja|短歌|12=draft}}.
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|12=draft}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏|12=draft}}, who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|12=draft}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>

The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==

The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tanka|ja|短歌|12=draft}}.
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|12=draft}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏|12=draft}}, who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|12=draft}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hichiriki|ja|篳篥|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Wagoto|ja|和琴|12=draft}}. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōkume no Mikoto|ja|大久米命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏|12=draft}} * {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft}} * {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭|12=draft}}

'''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡|12=draft}} found in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}. It is a {{ill|Gunka|ja|軍歌|12=draft}} (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=draft}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞|12=draft}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft}} to pacify regions such as {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}}. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=draft}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥|12=draft}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦|12=draft}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|12=draft}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺|12=draft}} in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft}}. While it fell into disuse after the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|ja|後土御門天皇|12=draft}} (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭|12=draft}} for {{ill|Emperor Ninkō|ja|仁孝天皇|12=draft}}'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|12=draft}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|12=draft}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|12=draft}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tanka|ja|短歌|12=draft}}.
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|12=draft}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏|12=draft}}, who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|12=draft}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hichiriki|ja|篳篥|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Wagoto|ja|和琴|12=draft}}. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōkume no Mikoto|ja|大久米命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏|12=draft}} * {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft}} * {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭|12=draft}}

revision.text:
'''久米歌'''あるいは '''来目歌'''（くめうた）は、日本の[[日本の上代文学史|上代]][[記紀]]歌謡の一つで、[[久米部]]の伝承した[[軍歌]]。[[久米舞]]の際に歌われる。
== 概要 == [[記紀]]の [[神武天皇]]条にある、 神武天皇が大和の[[兄猾]]（えうかし）・[[八十梟帥]]（やそたける）・[[兄磯城]]（えしき）・[[長髄彦]]（ながすねひこ）を征討したとき[[久米部]]がうたった歌であるという、[[大和国]]平定記事に組まれた宮廷歌曲群であり、『[[日本書紀]]』に8首、このうち6首が『[[古事記]]』にも書かれている<ref>『古事記』には神武天皇が[[ヒメタタライスズヒメ|伊須気余理比売]]に求婚した際に[[大久米命]]が仲介した和歌4首も書かれているが、一般的には久米歌とは呼ばれない。</ref>。[[ヤマト王権|大和王権]]に服した[[久米氏]]が、近衛軍団の[[伴造]]（とものみやつこ）、あるいは膳夫（かしわで、調理人）となり、戦闘後の酒宴の合唱と舞いとで、宮廷儀礼の場で[[大王]]（天皇）に忠誠を誓って奏したものである。
久米歌は勇壮な所作とともに、久米部の半農半猟的性格を表現したもので、久米舞と合わせて宮廷歌舞のひとつになり、儀式の際に演奏され、現在も[[宮内庁]]楽部で歌舞が行われている。
『日本書紀』には、「今、楽府に此の歌を奏ふ時は、なほ手量の大き小き、および音声の巨き細き有り。此は古の遺式なり」とある。
楽人による久米歌は、[[天平勝宝]]元年（[[749年]]）12月、[[東大寺]]で外来音楽とともに演奏されたのが文献初出で（『[[続日本紀]]』）、[[平安時代]]は、大嘗祭豊明節会の悠紀の舞として用いられ（主基の舞は吉志の舞）、[[後土御門天皇]]の代（在位1465年 - 1500年）以降、おこなわれず、[[1818年]]、[[仁孝天皇]]即位の大礼のときに再興され、以後、天皇即位の大礼で演奏されている。なお、[[1878年]]から[[1945年]]まで、[[紀元節]]の賀宴で豊明殿でおこなわれた。
== 考証 == [[高木市之助]]の説によると、久米歌には「[[撃ちてし止まむ]]（撃たずにおかぬものか）」という主題を伝えるために、
{{quotation|「粟生（あはふ）には 臭韮（かみら）一本（ひともと） そねが本 そね芽繋ぎて<ref name="歌">『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条</ref>」（訳：粟畑の臭いの強い韮が一本、その根本とその芽を繋ぐように）}}
あるいは {{quotation|「垣下（かきもと）に 植ゑし椒（はじかみ） 口ひひく<ref name="歌"/>」（『書紀』巻第三では「垣本（かきもと）に 植ゑし山椒（はじかみ） 口疼（ひび）く」<ref>『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀戊午年12月4日条</ref>）、（訳：柿の下に植えた山椒は口がひりひりする）}}
といった、日常の身近な体験が根本にあり、「吾や忘れじ」・「吾はや飢（ゑ）ぬ」という、「われ」の意志にもとづいて行動している点に特色がある。そこには氏族の長として氏族員を統率して戦闘を指揮しつつ、同時にその率いる氏族員と同じ農耕生活を営んでいる英雄時代の族長を感じさせるものがある、と述べられている。歌謡の形式として、長句と短句の組み合わせたり、各句の語数からして考察すると、長歌・短歌などの定型歌の成立とさほど遠くはない時期にできたものだと考えられ、 [[石母田正]]は歴史的にみて、 [[大和朝廷]]が抬頭し、周囲の国家を征服し、半島にまで勢力を拡大した [[5世紀]]頃（ [[雄略天皇]]時代）を想定している。
これに対し、 [[土橋寛]]はこれらの歌で「部下である久米部の気持ち」だけではなく、「族長の意志」も歌われており、「第三者の聞きて（天皇）」が想定され、天皇への久米氏の忠誠の誓約であることが本質であるとしている。 [[久米氏]]は [[大伴氏]]に統率された状態で、久米部を率いた中小伴造であり、大伴氏は天皇に従属する豪族である、という [[ヒエラルキー]]がある、というのである<ref name="久米">『日本の歴史1 神話から歴史へ』p336 - 344、井上光貞：著、中央公論社、1965年</ref>。
また、 [[倭王武]]の[[上表文]]に {{quotation|「昔自（よ）り祖彌（そでい）躬（みづか）ら甲冑を環（めぐ）らし、山川（さんせん）を跋渉（ばっしょう）し、寧処（ねいしょ）するに遑（いとま）あらず。東のかた毛人五十五国を征し、西のかた衆夷六十六国を服し、渡りて海の北の九十五国を平らぐ」<ref>『宋書』巻九十七夷蛮伝・倭国条</ref>}}
とあるように、この時代の天皇は軍を先頭に立って率いており、地方の豪族でも従わないものを討伐していた。にもかかわらず、朝廷の諸氏族も、地方の豪族も [[6世紀]]以降の史料においてすら、独立性と自由を確保し、はるかに活気に満ちた社会と歴史とを形成していた。そこにはこうした歌謡が描写している日本の[[英雄時代]]を想定することができる、というのが [[井上光貞]]の意見である<ref name="久米"/>。しかし、その後の歴史学・考古学の発展により、英雄時代の存在は現在では否定され、専制国家の成立していたことが明らかとなっている。
そのほか、久米歌には、大和政権服属以前に
{{quotation|「宇陀（うだ）の 高城（たかき）に 鴫羂（しぎわな）張る 吾が待つや 鴫は障（さや）らず いすくはし 鯨（くぢら）障る」<ref name="歌"/>}} など、山の民時代に狩りによせて敵を声高に笑った古い形と思われるものも存在する。
== 久米舞 == 久米舞の歌は、歌2人と伴奏である。
管のみでの音取（ねとり）ののち、歌「うたのたかきに...」を、参入音声（まいりおんじょう）と「前妻（こなみ）が...」の揚拍子（あげびょうし）（舞がある。ここのみリズムは拍節的）とに2分して、こののち歌「今はよ...」を、「讃声・歓声」と退出音声（まかでおんじょう）とに2分して、演奏する。
楽器は、龍笛1人、篳篥1人、和琴1人、そして琴持2人である。
舞は4人（かつては6人ないし20人）で、末額の冠をかぶり、笏と太刀をつけ、赤色の袍を着る。
構成は以下の通り。 * 音取（楽器:竜笛、篳篥） * 参入音声（歌:「うたのたかきに、」独唱）（歌:「しぎわなはる、...」斉唱）（楽器:和琴、笏拍子、竜笛、篳篥） * 舞 ** 揚拍子（歌:「こなみが、なこは」独唱）（歌:「さば、たちそはの...」斉唱）（楽器:和琴、笏拍子、竜笛、篳篥） ** （間奏）（楽器:和琴） ** （讃声）（歌:「いまはよ、いまはよ、」独唱）（楽器:和琴、笏拍子） ** （歓声）（歌:「あ、あ」斉唱）（楽器:和琴、笏拍子） * 退出音声（歌:「しやを、」独唱）（歌:「いまだにもよ、...」斉唱）（楽器:和琴、笏拍子、竜笛、篳篥）
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『古事記』完訳日本の古典1、[[小学館]]、1983年 * 『日本書紀』（一）、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年 * 『日本書紀』全現代語訳（上）、[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[宇治谷孟]]：訳、1988年 * 『新訂 [[魏志倭人伝]]・[[後漢書倭伝]]・[[宋書]]倭国伝・[[隋書]]倭国伝 －中国正史日本伝（1）』[[石原道博]]：編訳、岩波文庫、1951年 * 『倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本』全訳注、[[藤堂明保]]、[[竹田晃]]、影山輝國、講談社学術文庫、2010年 * 『日本の歴史1 神話から歴史へ』、井上光貞：著、中央公論社、1965年
== 関連項目 == * [[大久米命]] - [[久米氏]] - [[久米部]] * [[道臣命]] - [[大伴氏]] * [[神武天皇]] - [[神武東征]]（[[建国記念の日]]） * [[日本武尊]] * [[大嘗祭]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[久米歌]] to [[Kume no Uta]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
'''久米歌'''あるいは '''来目歌'''（くめうた）は、日本の[[日本の上代文学史|上代]][[記紀]]歌謡の一つで、[[久米部]]の伝承した[[軍歌]]。[[久米舞]]の際に歌われる。
== 概要 == [[記紀]]の [[神武天皇]]条にある、 神武天皇が大和の[[兄猾]]（えうかし）・[[八十梟帥]]（やそたける）・[[兄磯城]]（えしき）・[[長髄彦]]（ながすねひこ）を征討したとき[[久米部]]がうたった歌であるという、[[大和国]]平定記事に組まれた宮廷歌曲群であり、『[[日本書紀]]』に8首、このうち6首が『[[古事記]]』にも書かれている<ref>『古事記』には神武天皇が[[ヒメタタライスズヒメ|伊須気余理比売]]に求婚した際に[[大久米命]]が仲介した和歌4首も書かれているが、一般的には久米歌とは呼ばれない。</ref>。[[ヤマト王権|大和王権]]に服した[[久米氏]]が、近衛軍団の[[伴造]]（とものみやつこ）、あるいは膳夫（かしわで、調理人）となり、戦闘後の酒宴の合唱と舞いとで、宮廷儀礼の場で[[大王]]（天皇）に忠誠を誓って奏したものである。
久米歌は勇壮な所作とともに、久米部の半農半猟的性格を表現したもので、久米舞と合わせて宮廷歌舞のひとつになり、儀式の際に演奏され、現在も[[宮内庁]]楽部で歌舞が行われている。
『日本書紀』には、「今、楽府に此の歌を奏ふ時は、なほ手量の大き小き、および音声の巨き細き有り。此は古の遺式なり」とある。
楽人による久米歌は、[[天平勝宝]]元年（[[749年]]）12月、[[東大寺]]で外来音楽とともに演奏されたのが文献初出で（『[[続日本紀]]』）、[[平安時代]]は、大嘗祭豊明節会の悠紀の舞として用いられ（主基の舞は吉志の舞）、[[後土御門天皇]]の代（在位1465年 - 1500年）以降、おこなわれず、[[1818年]]、[[仁孝天皇]]即位の大礼のときに再興され、以後、天皇即位の大礼で演奏されている。なお、[[1878年]]から[[1945年]]まで、[[紀元節]]の賀宴で豊明殿でおこなわれた。
== 考証 == [[高木市之助]]の説によると、久米歌には「[[撃ちてし止まむ]]（撃たずにおかぬものか）」という主題を伝えるために、
{{quotation|「粟生（あはふ）には 臭韮（かみら）一本（ひともと） そねが本 そね芽繋ぎて<ref name="歌">『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条</ref>」（訳：粟畑の臭いの強い韮が一本、その根本とその芽を繋ぐように）}}
あるいは {{quotation|「垣下（かきもと）に 植ゑし椒（はじかみ） 口ひひく<ref name="歌"/>」（『書紀』巻第三では「垣本（かきもと）に 植ゑし山椒（はじかみ） 口疼（ひび）く」<ref>『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀戊午年12月4日条</ref>）、（訳：柿の下に植えた山椒は口がひりひりする）}}
といった、日常の身近な体験が根本にあり、「吾や忘れじ」・「吾はや飢（ゑ）ぬ」という、「われ」の意志にもとづいて行動している点に特色がある。そこには氏族の長として氏族員を統率して戦闘を指揮しつつ、同時にその率いる氏族員と同じ農耕生活を営んでいる英雄時代の族長を感じさせるものがある、と述べられている。歌謡の形式として、長句と短句の組み合わせたり、各句の語数からして考察すると、長歌・短歌などの定型歌の成立とさほど遠くはない時期にできたものだと考えられ、 [[石母田正]]は歴史的にみて、 [[大和朝廷]]が抬頭し、周囲の国家を征服し、半島にまで勢力を拡大した [[5世紀]]頃（ [[雄略天皇]]時代）を想定している。
これに対し、 [[土橋寛]]はこれらの歌で「部下である久米部の気持ち」だけではなく、「族長の意志」も歌われており、「第三者の聞きて（天皇）」が想定され、天皇への久米氏の忠誠の誓約であることが本質であるとしている。 [[久米氏]]は [[大伴氏]]に統率された状態で、久米部を率いた中小伴造であり、大伴氏は天皇に従属する豪族である、という [[ヒエラルキー]]がある、というのである<ref name="久米">『日本の歴史1 神話から歴史へ』p336 - 344、井上光貞：著、中央公論社、1965年</ref>。
また、 [[倭王武]]の[[上表文]]に {{quotation|「昔自（よ）り祖彌（そでい）躬（みづか）ら甲冑を環（めぐ）らし、山川（さんせん）を跋渉（ばっしょう）し、寧処（ねいしょ）するに遑（いとま）あらず。東のかた毛人五十五国を征し、西のかた衆夷六十六国を服し、渡りて海の北の九十五国を平らぐ」<ref>『宋書』巻九十七夷蛮伝・倭国条</ref>}}
とあるように、この時代の天皇は軍を先頭に立って率いており、地方の豪族でも従わないものを討伐していた。にもかかわらず、朝廷の諸氏族も、地方の豪族も [[6世紀]]以降の史料においてすら、独立性と自由を確保し、はるかに活気に満ちた社会と歴史とを形成していた。そこにはこうした歌謡が描写している日本の[[英雄時代]]を想定することができる、というのが [[井上光貞]]の意見である<ref name="久米"/>。しかし、その後の歴史学・考古学の発展により、英雄時代の存在は現在では否定され、専制国家の成立していたことが明らかとなっている。
そのほか、久米歌には、大和政権服属以前に
{{quotation|「宇陀（うだ）の 高城（たかき）に 鴫羂（しぎわな）張る 吾が待つや 鴫は障（さや）らず いすくはし 鯨（くぢら）障る」<ref name="歌"/>}} など、山の民時代に狩りによせて敵を声高に笑った古い形と思われるものも存在する。
== 久米舞 == 久米舞の歌は、歌2人と伴奏である。
管のみでの音取（ねとり）ののち、歌「うたのたかきに...」を、参入音声（まいりおんじょう）と「前妻（こなみ）が...」の揚拍子（あげびょうし）（舞がある。ここのみリズムは拍節的）とに2分して、こののち歌「今はよ...」を、「讃声・歓声」と退出音声（まかでおんじょう）とに2分して、演奏する。
楽器は、龍笛1人、篳篥1人、和琴1人、そして琴持2人である。
舞は4人（かつては6人ないし20人）で、末額の冠をかぶり、笏と太刀をつけ、赤色の袍を着る。
構成は以下の通り。 * 音取（楽器:竜笛、篳篥） * 参入音声（歌:「うたのたかきに、」独唱）（歌:「しぎわなはる、...」斉唱）（楽器:和琴、笏拍子、竜笛、篳篥） * 舞 ** 揚拍子（歌:「こなみが、なこは」独唱）（歌:「さば、たちそはの...」斉唱）（楽器:和琴、笏拍子、竜笛、篳篥） ** （間奏）（楽器:和琴） ** （讃声）（歌:「いまはよ、いまはよ、」独唱）（楽器:和琴、笏拍子） ** （歓声）（歌:「あ、あ」斉唱）（楽器:和琴、笏拍子） * 退出音声（歌:「しやを、」独唱）（歌:「いまだにもよ、...」斉唱）（楽器:和琴、笏拍子、竜笛、篳篥）
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『古事記』完訳日本の古典1、[[小学館]]、1983年 * 『日本書紀』（一）、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年 * 『日本書紀』全現代語訳（上）、[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[宇治谷孟]]：訳、1988年 * 『新訂 [[魏志倭人伝]]・[[後漢書倭伝]]・[[宋書]]倭国伝・[[隋書]]倭国伝 －中国正史日本伝（1）』[[石原道博]]：編訳、岩波文庫、1951年 * 『倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本』全訳注、[[藤堂明保]]、[[竹田晃]]、影山輝國、講談社学術文庫、2010年 * 『日本の歴史1 神話から歴史へ』、井上光貞：著、中央公論社、1965年
== 関連項目 == * [[大久米命]] - [[久米氏]] - [[久米部]] * [[道臣命]] - [[大伴氏]] * [[神武天皇]] - [[神武東征]]（[[建国記念の日]]） * [[日本武尊]] * [[大嘗祭]]

revision.text:
'''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kayo|qq=}} found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. It is a [[:en:War song|Gunka]] (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumemai|qq=}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] to pacify regions such as [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eukashi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaso Takeru|qq=}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eshiki|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagazunehiko|qq=}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|qq=}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]. While it fell into disuse after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado]] (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] for [[:en:Emperor Ninkō|Emperor Ninkō]]'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigensetsu|qq=}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyomeiden|qq=}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takagi Ichinosuke|qq=}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōka|qq=}} and [[:en:Tanka|Tanka]].
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|qq=}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|qq=}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|qq=}}, [[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]], and [[:en:Yamatogoto|Wagoto]]. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōkume no Mikoto|ja|大久米命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkume no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} * [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]] * [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] * [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Japan-culture-stub}} {{Music-stub}}

'''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kayo|qq=}} found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. It is a [[:en:War song|Gunka]] (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumemai|qq=}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] to pacify regions such as [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eukashi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaso Takeru|qq=}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eshiki|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagazunehiko|qq=}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|qq=}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]. While it fell into disuse after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado]] (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] for [[:en:Emperor Ninkō|Emperor Ninkō]]'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigensetsu|qq=}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyomeiden|qq=}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takagi Ichinosuke|qq=}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōka|qq=}} and [[:en:Tanka|Tanka]].
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|qq=}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|qq=}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|qq=}}, [[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]], and [[:en:Yamatogoto|Wagoto]]. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).

<!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--> [[Category:久米氏]]

'''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kayo|qq=}} found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. It is a [[:en:War song|Gunka]] (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumemai|qq=}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] to pacify regions such as [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eukashi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaso Takeru|qq=}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eshiki|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagazunehiko|qq=}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|qq=}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]. While it fell into disuse after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado]] (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] for [[:en:Emperor Ninkō|Emperor Ninkō]]'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigensetsu|qq=}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyomeiden|qq=}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takagi Ichinosuke|qq=}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōka|qq=}} and [[:en:Tanka|Tanka]].
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|qq=}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|qq=}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|qq=}}, [[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]], and [[:en:Yamatogoto|Wagoto]]. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==

'''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kayo|qq=}} found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. It is a [[:en:War song|Gunka]] (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumemai|qq=}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] to pacify regions such as [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eukashi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaso Takeru|qq=}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eshiki|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagazunehiko|qq=}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|qq=}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]. While it fell into disuse after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado]] (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] for [[:en:Emperor Ninkō|Emperor Ninkō]]'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigensetsu|qq=}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyomeiden|qq=}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takagi Ichinosuke|qq=}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōka|qq=}} and [[:en:Tanka|Tanka]].
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|qq=}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|qq=}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|qq=}}, [[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]], and [[:en:Yamatogoto|Wagoto]]. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōkume no Mikoto|ja|大久米命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkume no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} * [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]] * [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] * [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Japan-culture-stub}} {{Music-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|久米歌|version=100772756|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Traditional Japanese music]] [[Category:Gagaku]] [[Category:Kagura]]
[[Category:Completion tier 2]]
}}

'''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kayo|qq=}} found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. It is a [[:en:War song|Gunka]] (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumemai|qq=}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] to pacify regions such as [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eukashi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaso Takeru|qq=}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eshiki|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagazunehiko|qq=}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|qq=}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]. While it fell into disuse after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado]] (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] for [[:en:Emperor Ninkō|Emperor Ninkō]]'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigensetsu|qq=}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyomeiden|qq=}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takagi Ichinosuke|qq=}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōka|qq=}} and [[:en:Tanka|Tanka]].
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|qq=}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>

While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>

Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).

He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|qq=}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|qq=}}, [[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]], and [[:en:Yamatogoto|Wagoto]]. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.

[[sn:Kume no Uta]] [[ja:久米歌]] [[en:Kume no Uta]]
<!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--> [[Category:久米氏]] [[Category:Saeki clan]] [[Category:Otomo clan]] [[Category:Japanese traditional music]] [[Category:Dances of Japan]] [[Category:歌謡]]

'''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kayo|qq=}} found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. It is a [[:en:War song|Gunka]] (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumemai|qq=}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] to pacify regions such as [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eukashi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaso Takeru|qq=}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eshiki|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagazunehiko|qq=}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|qq=}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]. While it fell into disuse after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado]] (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] for [[:en:Emperor Ninkō|Emperor Ninkō]]'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigensetsu|qq=}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyomeiden|qq=}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takagi Ichinosuke|qq=}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōka|qq=}} and [[:en:Tanka|Tanka]].
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|qq=}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|qq=}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|qq=}}, [[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]], and [[:en:Yamatogoto|Wagoto]]. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōkume no Mikoto|ja|大久米命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkume no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} * [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]] * [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] * [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]]

It is a [[:en:War song|Gunka]] (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumemai|qq=}} dance.

According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eukashi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaso Takeru|qq=}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eshiki|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagazunehiko|qq=}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマト王権}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]. While it fell into disuse after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado]] (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] for [[:en:Emperor Ninkō|Emperor Ninkō]]'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigensetsu|qq=}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyomeiden|qq=}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takagi Ichinosuke|qq=}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōka|qq=}} and [[:en:Tanka|Tanka]].
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|qq=}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 龍笛}}, [[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]], and [[:en:Yamatogoto|Wagoto]]. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōkume no Mikoto|ja|大久米命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkume no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} * [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]] * [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] * [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]]
