The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōka|qq=}} and [[:en:Tanka|Tanka]].
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|qq=}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 龍笛}}, [[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]], and [[:en:Yamatogoto|Wagoto]]. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōkume no Mikoto|ja|大久米命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkume no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} * [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]] * [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] * [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q55529556]] Wikidata name: Kume-uta [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kayo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 歌}} found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. It is a [[:en:War song|Gunka]] (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumemai|qq=}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] to pacify regions such as [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eukashi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaso Takeru|qq=}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eshiki|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagazunehiko|qq=}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|qq=|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|qq=}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]. While it fell into disuse after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado]] (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] for [[:en:Emperor Ninkō|Emperor Ninkō]]'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigensetsu|qq=}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyomeiden|qq=}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takagi Ichinosuke|qq=}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōka|qq=}} and [[:en:Tanka|Tanka]].
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|qq=}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 龍笛}}, [[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]], and [[:en:Yamatogoto|Wagoto]]. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōkume no Mikoto|ja|大久米命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkume no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} * [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]] * [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] * [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]]

The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 龍笛|1=ryūteki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1760249}}, [[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]], and [[:en:Yamatogoto|Wagoto]]. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.

<!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--> [[Category:Kume Clan]] [[Category:Saeki clan]] [[Category:Otomo clan]] [[Category:Japanese traditional music]] [[Category:Dances of Japan]] [[Category:Kayo]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q55529556}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米歌]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55529556]] Wikidata name: Kume-uta [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kayo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 歌|1=song|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7366}} found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. It is a [[:en:War song|Gunka]] (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumemai|qq=|1=Kumemai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55529628}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] to pacify regions such as [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eukashi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaso Takeru|qq=|1=Yasotakeru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390617}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eshiki|qq=|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagazunehiko|qq=|1=Nagasunehiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4922278}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|qq=|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|qq=|1=Yamato Kinship|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]. While it fell into disuse after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado]] (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] for [[:en:Emperor Ninkō|Emperor Ninkō]]'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigensetsu|qq=}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyomeiden|qq=|1=Hōmeiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11633731}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takagi Ichinosuke|qq=|1=Ichinosuke Takagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11670045}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōka|qq=|1=chōka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q773298}} and [[:en:Tanka|Tanka]].
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|qq=|1=Yutaka Tsuchihashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423709}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 龍笛|1=ryūteki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1760249}}, [[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]], and [[:en:Yamatogoto|Wagoto]]. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==

The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米歌]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55529556]] Wikidata name: Kume-uta [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kayo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 歌|1=song|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7366}} found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. It is a [[:en:War song|Gunka]] (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumemai|1=Kumemai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55529628}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] to pacify regions such as [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eukashi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaso Takeru|1=Yasotakeru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390617}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eshiki|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagazunehiko|1=Nagasunehiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4922278}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|1=Yamato Kinship|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]. While it fell into disuse after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado]] (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] for [[:en:Emperor Ninkō|Emperor Ninkō]]'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigensetsu|qq=}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyomeiden|1=Hōmeiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11633731}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takagi Ichinosuke|1=Ichinosuke Takagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11670045}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōka|1=chōka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q773298}} and [[:en:Tanka|Tanka]].
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|1=Yutaka Tsuchihashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423709}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 龍笛|1=ryūteki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1760249}}, [[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]], and [[:en:Yamatogoto|Wagoto]]. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōkume no Mikoto|ja|大久米命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkume no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} * [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]] * [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] * [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]]

<!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--><!--Kume no Uta--> [[Category:Kume Clan]] [[Category:Saeki clan]] [[Category:Otomo clan]] [[Category:Japanese traditional music]] [[Category:Dances of Japan]] [[Category:Kayo]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q55529556}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米歌]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55529556]] Wikidata name: Kume-uta [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kayo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 歌|1=song|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7366}} found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. It is a [[:en:War song|Gunka]] (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumemai|1=Kumemai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55529628}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] to pacify regions such as [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eukashi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaso Takeru|1=Yasotakeru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390617}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eshiki|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagazunehiko|1=Nagasunehiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4922278}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|1=Yamato Kinship|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]. While it fell into disuse after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado]] (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] for [[:en:Emperor Ninkō|Emperor Ninkō]]'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigensetsu|qq=}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyomeiden|1=Hōmeiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11633731}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takagi Ichinosuke|1=Ichinosuke Takagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11670045}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōka|1=chōka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q773298}} and [[:en:Tanka|Tanka]].
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|1=Yutaka Tsuchihashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423709}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 龍笛|1=ryūteki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1760249}}, [[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]], and [[:en:Yamatogoto|Wagoto]]. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米歌]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55529556]] Wikidata name: Kume-uta [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kayo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 歌|1=song|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7366}} found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. It is a [[:en:War song|Gunka]] (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumemai|1=Kumemai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55529628}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] to pacify regions such as [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eukashi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaso Takeru|1=Yasotakeru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390617}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eshiki|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagazunehiko|1=Nagasunehiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4922278}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|1=Yamato Kinship|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]. While it fell into disuse after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado]] (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] for [[:en:Emperor Ninkō|Emperor Ninkō]]'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigensetsu|qq=}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyomeiden|1=Hōmeiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11633731}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takagi Ichinosuke|1=Ichinosuke Takagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11670045}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōka|1=chōka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q773298}} and [[:en:Tanka|Tanka]].
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|1=Yutaka Tsuchihashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423709}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 龍笛|1=ryūteki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1760249}}, [[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]], and [[:en:Yamatogoto|Wagoto]]. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōkume no Mikoto|ja|大久米命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkume no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} * [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]] * [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] * [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Japan-culture-stub}} {{Music-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米歌]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55529556]] Wikidata name: Kume-uta [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kayo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 歌|1=song|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7366}} found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. It is a [[:en:War song|Gunka]] (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumemai|1=Kumemai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55529628}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] to pacify regions such as [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eukashi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaso Takeru|1=Yasotakeru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390617}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eshiki|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagazunehiko|1=Nagasunehiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4922278}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|1=Yamato Kinship|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]. While it fell into disuse after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado]] (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] for [[:en:Emperor Ninkō|Emperor Ninkō]]'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigensetsu|qq=}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyomeiden|1=Hōmeiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11633731}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takagi Ichinosuke|1=Ichinosuke Takagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11670045}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōka|1=chōka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q773298}} and [[:en:Tanka|Tanka]].
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|1=Yutaka Tsuchihashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423709}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 龍笛|1=ryūteki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1760249}}, [[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]], and [[:en:Yamatogoto|Wagoto]]. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōkume no Mikoto|ja|大久米命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkume no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} * [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]] * [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] * [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米歌]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米歌]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米歌]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55529556]] Wikidata name: Kume-uta [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kayo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 歌|1=song|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7366}} found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. It is a [[:en:War song|Gunka]] (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumemai|1=Kumemai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55529628}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] to pacify regions such as [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eukashi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaso Takeru|1=Yasotakeru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390617}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eshiki|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagazunehiko|1=Nagasunehiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4922278}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|1=Yamato Kinship|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]. While it fell into disuse after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado]] (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] for [[:en:Emperor Ninkō|Emperor Ninkō]]'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigensetsu|qq=}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyomeiden|1=Hōmeiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11633731}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takagi Ichinosuke|1=Ichinosuke Takagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11670045}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōka|1=chōka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q773298}} and [[:en:Tanka|Tanka]].
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|1=Yutaka Tsuchihashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423709}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 龍笛|1=ryūteki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1760249}}, [[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]], and [[:en:Yamatogoto|Wagoto]]. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōkume no Mikoto|ja|大久米命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkume no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} * [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]] * [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] * [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米歌]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米歌]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米歌]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55529556]] Wikidata name: Kume-uta [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kayo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 歌|1=song|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7366}} found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. It is a [[:en:War song|Gunka]] (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumemai|1=Kumemai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55529628}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] to pacify regions such as [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eukashi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaso Takeru|1=Yasotakeru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390617}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eshiki|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagazunehiko|1=Nagasunehiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4922278}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|1=Yamato Kinship|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]. While it fell into disuse after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado]] (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] for [[:en:Emperor Ninkō|Emperor Ninkō]]'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigensetsu|qq=}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyomeiden|1=Hōmeiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11633731}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takagi Ichinosuke|1=Ichinosuke Takagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11670045}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōka|1=chōka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q773298}} and [[:en:Tanka|Tanka]].
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|1=Yutaka Tsuchihashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423709}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 龍笛|1=ryūteki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1760249}}, [[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]], and [[:en:Yamatogoto|Wagoto]]. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōkume no Mikoto|ja|大久米命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkume no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} * [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]] * [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] * [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Japan-culture-stub}} {{Music-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|久米歌|version=100772756|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:久米歌]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米歌]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米歌]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米歌]]
'''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kayo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 歌|1=song|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7366}} found in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}. It is a {{ill|War song|en|War song|WD=Q16194930|lt=Gunka}} (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumemai|1=Kumemai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55529628}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}} to pacify regions such as {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eukashi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaso Takeru|1=Yasotakeru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390617}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eshiki|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagazunehiko|1=Nagasunehiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4922278}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|1=Yamato Kinship|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at {{ill|Tōdai-ji|en|Tōdai-ji|WD=Q460367|lt=Tōdai-ji}} in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}. While it fell into disuse after the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|en|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|WD=Q380623|lt=Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado}} (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|WD=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai}} for {{ill|Emperor Ninkō|en|Emperor Ninkō|WD=Q312351|lt=Emperor Ninkō}}'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigensetsu|qq=}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyomeiden|1=Hōmeiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11633731}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takagi Ichinosuke|1=Ichinosuke Takagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11670045}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōka|1=chōka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q773298}} and {{ill|Tanka|en|Tanka|WD=Q258331|lt=Tanka}}.
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|1=Yutaka Tsuchihashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423709}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan}}, who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 龍笛|1=ryūteki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1760249}}, {{ill|Hichiriki|en|Hichiriki|WD=Q425748|lt=Hichiriki}}, and {{ill|Yamatogoto|en|Yamatogoto|WD=Q3571466|lt=Wagoto}}. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōkume no Mikoto|ja|大久米命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkume no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan}} * {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}} * {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|WD=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai}}

On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|1=Yutaka Tsuchihashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423709}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan}}, who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 龍笛|1=ryūteki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1760249}}, {{ill|Hichiriki|en|Hichiriki|WD=Q425748|lt=Hichiriki}}, and {{ill|Yamatogoto|en|Yamatogoto|WD=Q3571466|lt=Wagoto}}. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōkume no Mikoto|ja|大久米命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkume no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan}} * {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}} * {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|WD=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米歌]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米歌]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米歌]]
'''Kumeuta''' or '''Kumeno Uta''' (久米歌・来目歌, "Songs of Kume") is a form of ancient Japanese {{ill|kayo|ja|歌謡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kayo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 歌|1=Song|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7366}} found in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}. It is a {{ill|War song|en|War song|WD=Q16194930|lt=Gunka|1=War song}} (military song) handed down by the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} and traditionally performed during the {{ill|Kumemai|ja|久米舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumemai|1=Kume-Mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55529628}} dance.
== Overview == The songs are associated with the legendary expeditions of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} to pacify regions such as {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}}. According to the chronicles, the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} sang these songs during battles against figures like {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eukashi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yaso Takeru|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaso Takeru|1=Yasotakeru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390617}}, {{ill|Eshiki|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eshiki|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}, and {{ill|Nagazunehiko|ja|長髄彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagazunehiko|1=Nagatsuhiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4922278}}. These songs, part of a repertoire used in court ceremonies, are preserved in eight instances in the *Nihon Shoki*, with six of them also addearing in the *Kojiki*.<ref>*Kojiki*, middlevolume, Emperor Jimmu section.</ref>
The songs were likely performed by members of the Kumebe, who served the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|1=Yamato Kingship|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}} as both warriors and stewards. Their performances during post-battle banquets, accompanied by dance, became a form of courtly ritual affirming loyalty to the emperor. The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at {{ill|Tōdai-ji|en|Tōdai-ji|WD=Q460367|lt=Tōdai-ji|1=Tōdai-ji}} in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}}. While it fell into disuse after the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|en|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|WD=Q380623|lt=Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|1=Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado}} (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|WD=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai|1=Daijosai}} for {{ill|Emperor Ninkō|en|Emperor Ninkō|WD=Q312351|lt=Emperor Ninkō|1=Emperor Ninkō}}'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigensetsu|qq=}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyomeiden|1=Hōmeiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11633731}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takagi Ichinosuke|1=Ichinosuke Takagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11670045}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōka|1=chōka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q773298}} and {{ill|Tanka|en|Tanka|WD=Q258331|lt=Tanka|1=Tanka}}.
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|1=Yutaka Tsuchihashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423709}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan|1=Ōtomo clan (ancient)}}, who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 龍笛|1=Ryūteki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1760249}}, {{ill|Hichiriki|en|Hichiriki|WD=Q425748|lt=Hichiriki|1=Hichiriki}}, and {{ill|Yamatogoto|en|Yamatogoto|WD=Q3571466|lt=Wagoto|1=Yamatogoto}}. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōkume no Mikoto|ja|大久米命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkume no Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumebe|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan|1=Ōtomo clan (ancient)}} * {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} * {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|WD=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai|1=Daijosai}}

The martial themes of the songs align with the half-agricultural, half-hunting lifestyle of the Kumebe, expressed through the vigorous movements of the Kumemai.
The *Nihon Shoki* notes, "Even now, when musicians perform this song, they follow ancient customs regarding hand gestures and vocalization, preserving the traditions of old."
The Kumemai is documented as having been performed alongside foreign music at {{ill|Tōdai-ji|en|Tōdai-ji|WD=Q460367|lt=Tōdai-ji|1=Tōdai-ji}} in 749 (*Tenpyō Shōhō* 1) according to the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}}. While it fell into disuse after the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|en|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|WD=Q380623|lt=Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|1=Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado}} (1465–1500), the Kumemai was revived during the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|WD=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai|1=Daijosai}} for {{ill|Emperor Ninkō|en|Emperor Ninkō|WD=Q312351|lt=Emperor Ninkō|1=Emperor Ninkō}}'s enthronement in 1818. Subsequently, it was performed during imperial enthronement ceremonies and at {{ill|Kigensetsu|ja|紀元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigensetsu|qq=}} banquets in the {{ill|Toyomeiden|ja|豊明殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyomeiden|1=Hōmeiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11633731}} from 1878 to 1945.
== Analysis == According to {{ill|Takagi Ichinosuke|ja|高木市之助|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takagi Ichinosuke|1=Ichinosuke Takagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11670045}}, Kumeuta conveys themes of determination and martial valor, as epitomized by lines like:
{{quotation|"In the millet fields, a single stalk of smelly chive—its root and shoots are tied together." (*Kojiki*)}}
and:
{{quotation|"Under the hedge grows a pungent pedder plant, stinging the mouth." (*Nihon Shoki*, Volume 3)<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Jimmu pre-enthronement, fifth year of the dog, 12th month, 4th day.</ref>}}
Takagi suggests that these simple, everyday images express personal resolve and agency ("I shall not forget" or "I have starved enough"). These themes resonate with a vision of clan leaders who, while leading in battle, shared in the agricultural lives of their clansmen. The combination of long and short verses suggests the songs originated around the same period as the formation of structured poetry forms like {{ill|Chōka|ja|長歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōka|1=chōka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q773298}} and {{ill|Tanka|en|Tanka|WD=Q258331|lt=Tanka|1=Tanka}}.
On the other hand, historian {{ill|Dobashi Hiroshi|ja|土橋寛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dobashi Hiroshi|1=Yutaka Tsuchihashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423709}} argues that the songs are not merely expressions of the Kumebe's feelings but also affirm the loyalty of the Kumebe to the emperor. He highlights the hierarchical relationship wherein the Kumebe were subordinate to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan|1=Ōtomo clan (ancient)}}, who in turn served the emperor.<ref name="Kume">Inoue Mitsusada, *Myth to History*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1965.</ref>
The {{ill|Song of the Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song of the Empress Jingū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a later document, records similar themes of the emperor directly leading troops and subduing rebellious clans during Japan's formative periods. While earlier interpretations posited a heroic age, modern research has clarified that a centralized monarchy had already emerged.<ref name="Kume"/>
== The Kumemai == The Kumemai is performed by two singers accompanied by instruments, including a {{ill|Ryūteki|ja|竜笛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūteki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 龍笛|1=Ryūteki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1760249}}, {{ill|Hichiriki|en|Hichiriki|WD=Q425748|lt=Hichiriki|1=Hichiriki}}, and {{ill|Yamatogoto|en|Yamatogoto|WD=Q3571466|lt=Wagoto|1=Yamatogoto}}. Four dancers, clad in crimson robes and carrying ritual swords, execute the choreography.
The structure includes: * '''Opening Prelude''' (instrumental) * '''Entrance Songs''' (vocal and instrumental) * '''Dance Sections''', featuring chanted verses and instrumental interludes. * '''Closing Prelude''' (instrumental and vocal exit).
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * *Kojiki* (Complete Translation), Shōgakukan, 1983. * *Nihon Shoki* (Volume 1), Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Mitsusada, Inoue. *Myth to History*. Chūō Kōronsha, 1965. * Moriyasu, Tanabe. "Court Music and Rituals in Ancient Japan," Iwanami Bunko, 2010.
